perturbation.original_text: 3.5. When this agreement comes to an end or written demand has been sent, confidential information must be returned to the other party , within 5 (five) business days of the expiry date or the date that the demand was received. Confidential information held in electronic format mus t be destroyed or deleted to protect its confidentiality .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5. When this agreement comes to an end, confidential information must be returned to the other party. Confidential information held in electronic format may be retained.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement to destroy or delete confidential information held in electronic format and removes the timeframe for returning the information. This creates a legal risk, as it could violate data protection laws that mandate secure disposal of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA), South Africa
perturbation.law_citation: Section 14(2)(c) of the Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA mandates that responsible parties must destroy or delete personal information as soon as reasonably practicable once the purpose for which it was collected has been achieved, or the responsible party is no longer authorised to retain the information. By removing the obligation to delete electronic confidential information, the modified clause contradicts this requirement and creates a risk of non-compliance with POPIA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which comme
perturbation.justification: The modification omits the requirement to destroy or delete confidential information held in electronic format, contradicting the principle that personal information should not be retained indefinitely, especially once its purpose has been fulfilled, according to the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippet 2, describes that the PoPI Act governs data protection and privacy in South Africa and aims to regulate the right to privacy. By removing the obligation to delete electronic confidential information, the modification increases the risk of non-compliance with data protection laws, specifically the right to privacy. This establishes a clear legal contradiction as defined by an 'Omission Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: 3.8. The Courts of the Republic of South Africa have jurisdiction over any dispute resulting from this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.8. The parties agree to resolve any dispute resulting from this agreement through binding arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces court jurisdiction with binding arbitration. While arbitration is a valid dispute resolution method, removing the right to access the courts of the Republic of South Africa, especially for potentially vulnerable parties, could violate constitutional rights to access justice. This clause presents a legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 34 of the Constitution of the Republic of South Africa, 1996
perturbation.law_citation: Section 34 of the Constitution of the Republic of South Africa, 1996
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 34 of the Constitution guarantees everyone the right to have any dispute that can be resolved by the application of law decided in a fair public hearing before a court or, where appropriate, another independent and impartial tribunal or forum. While arbitration is permissible, a clause that effectively prevents access to courts altogether, especially without clear and informed consent, could be challenged as a violation of this constitutional right. Omitting the specification that the Courts of South Africa have jurisdiction, in favour of mandatory arbitration, introduces a risk as some arbitrations may be costly and prevent access to justice.

perturbation.justification: The modification replaces court jurisdiction with binding arbitration, potentially violating the constitutional right to access justice as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Omitting the courts of South Africa having jurisdiction introduces risk, as binding arbitration may be costly and prevent access to justice. This aligns with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, where a legally mandated consumer protection is omitted, making the contract non-compliant. Since there are no law snippets available, it is difficult to make a full evaluation but based on the 'Law Explanation' and the 'Perturbation Explanation' there is a reasonable ground to believe that the contract violates the constitutional right to access justice. Therefore, the modification contradicts the existing law.

perturbation.original_text: 3.12. The Parties may send any non-legal documents, notices or other communication via Email, but must attach a “read receipt” to each mail sent, to provide confirmation that t he communication has been received and read.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.12. The Parties may send any non-legal documents, notices or other communication via Email.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for a "read receipt" for email communication. While seemingly minor, in legal contexts, establishing proof of communication is crucial. Omitting the 'read receipt' requirement could hinder the ability to demonstrate that a party received important notices, especially if the contract deals with time-sensitive obligations. This raises a legal risk related to proof of service.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Electronic Communications and Transactions Act 25 of 2002 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 23(b) of the Electronic Communications and Transactions Act 25 of 2002
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 23 of the Electronic Communications and Transactions Act acknowledges data messages as a form of evidence. While not explicitly mandating read receipts, the act recognizes the importance of establishing receipt and acknowledgement of electronic communication. By removing the requirement for a read receipt, the clause makes it more difficult to prove that a notice was received and read, potentially undermining legal certainty and creating disputes regarding notification obligations. This increases the burden of proof and potential litigation costs.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Electronic Communications and Transactions Act, 2002 Electronic Communications and Transactions Act, 2002 No. 25 of 2002 (English text signed by the President.) (Assented to 31 July 2002.) ACT To provide for the facilitation and regulation of electronic communications and transactions; to provide for the development of a national e-strategy for the Republic; to promote universal access to electronic communications and transac­tions and the use of electronic transactions by SMMEs; to provide for human resource development in electronic transactions; to prevent abuse of information systems; to encourage the use of e-government services; and to provide for matters connected therewith. BE IT ENACTED by the Parliament of the Republic of South Africa, as follows:­— ARRANGEMENT OF SECTIONS Sections CHAPTER I INTERPRETATION, OBJECTS AND APPLICATION 1. Definitions 2. Objects of Act 3. Interpretation 4. Sphere of application CHAPTER II MAXIMISING BENEFITS AND POLICY FRAMEWORK Part 1 National e-strategy 5. National e-strategy 6. Universal access 7. Previously disadvantaged persons and communities 8. Development of human resources 9. SMMEs Part 2 Electronic transactions policy 10. Electronic transactions policy CHAPTER III FACILITATING ELECTRONIC TRANSACTIONS Part 1 Legal requirements for data messages 11. Legal recognition of data messages 12. Writing 13. Signature 14. Original 15. Admissibility and evidential weight of data messages 16. Retention 17. Production of document or information 18. Notarisation, acknowledgement and certification 19. Other requirements 20. Automated transactions Part 2 Communication of data messages 21. Variation by agreement between parties 22. Formation and validity of agreements 23. Time and place of communications, dispatch and receipt 24. Expression of intent or other statement 25. Attribution of data messages to originator 26. Acknowledgement of receipt of data message CHAPTER IV E-GOVERNMENT SERVICES 27. Acceptance of electronic filing and issuing of documents 28. Requirements may be specified
perturbation.justification: The original text required a read receipt for email communication, which provides proof of delivery. The changed text removes this requirement. The Law Explanation states that removing the read receipt makes it more difficult to prove that a notice was received and read, potentially undermining legal certainty. Scraped Law Snippet 2, the Electronic Communications and Transactions Act, generally acknowledges electronic communications, but it doesn't negate the need for proof of receipt when specified in a contract. Removing the read receipt weakens the ability to demonstrate that a party received important notices. This omission creates a legal contradiction by potentially hindering proof of service, especially where the contract involves time-sensitive obligations as explained in the Perturbation Explanation.
